meta.section: Exchange — Mail Flow Hardening + Non-Email-Security Baseline Items
meta.source: Inforcer baseline export + Microsoft Learn. VERIFICATION playbook: settings are the documented baseline values; most are org-wide Exchange Online settings read with Get-OrganizationConfig (or Get-TransportConfig / Get-TransportRule). Several rows are PowerShell-only with no portal page — for those the steps are cmdlet-based and no Open-portal button appears.
generalNotes: Most of these are single org-wide settings surfaced by Get-OrganizationConfig. To verify a value, connect to Exchange Online PowerShell and run: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object <FieldName> — then compare to the baseline value and record any drift.

Many rows in the 'Non-Email-Security Baseline Items' category are UX/feature toggles with no security impact — they're documented for completeness. The security-relevant ones are flagged in their steps (Alert External Emails, Security & Anti-Spoofing / RejectDirectSend, Authentication & Session, Compliance & Auditing / Customer Lockbox, Microsoft Bookings, Delayed Delicensing, Archive & Retention).

Where the Inforcer review found a documentation gap or an unresolved export discrepancy, the setting is reported as configured only — verify directly against the tenant before relying on it in a client conversation.

requiredSettings.label: Allow list
requiredSettings.value: Not present in export — confirm it isn't unexpectedly excluding trusted partners

steps: In the Microsoft 365 admin center (admin.microsoft.com), go to Settings > Org settings > Security & privacy > External sender tagging, and confirm it's turned on.
Or verify in Exchange Online PowerShell: Get-ExternalInOutlook (confirm Enabled = True and review AllowList).
This is the header-area 'External' tag; it complements the 'Inforcer - Alert External Emails' body banner — genuine defense-in-depth, not a duplicate. Record any drift.
In the Exchange admin center (admin.exchange.microsoft.com), go to Settings > Mail flow and review the org-wide SMTP AUTH setting.
Verify in PowerShell: Get-TransportConfig | Select-Object SmtpClientAuthenticationDisabled — confirm True (legacy SMTP AUTH off org-wide).

SmtpClientAuthenticationDisabled = True is deliberate hardening (not the historical default). Confirm no Conditional Access exclusion reopens the same legacy-auth path. Any mailbox that still needs SMTP AUTH must be enabled per-mailbox as a documented exception.

requiredSettings.label: Action
requiredSettings.value: Prepend an HTML caution banner to the message body

requiredSettings.label: HTML disclaimer fallback action
requiredSettings.value: Wrap — DEVIATION (Microsoft recommends Reject; see steps)

steps: In the Exchange admin center (admin.exchange.microsoft.com), go to Mail flow > Rules and open the 'Inforcer - Alert External Emails' rule.
Confirm the rule applies when the sender is outside and the recipient is inside the organization, and that it prepends the HTML disclaimer banner.

CRITICAL: the HTML disclaimer fallback action is 'Wrap'. Microsoft warns against Wrap on rules affecting external senders because it interferes with Safe Attachments scanning for the affected messages. Recommended fix: change the fallback action to Reject. Confirm with the client first — Reject bounces the message instead of delivering it wrapped (a real behavior change).
Confirm rule priority/ordering against the full rule set: Get-TransportRule | Format-Table Name,Priority,State.
In the Exchange admin center, go to Settings > Mail flow and review the MailTips panel.
Verify in PowerShell: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object MailTipsAllTipsEnabled, MailTipsExternalRecipientsTipsEnabled, MailTipsLargeAudienceThreshold.

MailTipsExternalRecipientsTipsEnabled = True is deliberate hardening (defaults to False) — the compose-time complement to the external-email banner, warning senders before they send externally. Record any drift.

requiredSettings.label: Idle session timeout (Outlook on the web)

steps: Confirm modern auth in the Microsoft 365 admin center: Settings > Org settings > Modern authentication (or Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object OAuth2ClientProfileEnabled).
CRITICAL: if OAuth2ClientProfileEnabled were False it would silently undermine the SMTP AUTH blocking above and weaken CA/MFA enforcement on mail clients — verify it's True.

Confirm the idle session timeout in Microsoft 365 admin center: Settings > Org settings > Security & privacy > Idle session timeout (protects unattended Outlook-on-the-web sessions on shared devices).
Note: EnforceExoAppRbacPermissions is a separate mechanism from EWS access control — don't conflate the two.

requiredSettings.label: Reject Direct Send
requiredSettings.value: True — NOT the default (ships False; public preview feature)

requiredSettings.label: Mask client IP in received headers

steps: PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object RejectDirectSend, MaskClientIpInReceivedHeadersEnabled.

MOST SIGNIFICANT FINDING IN THE REVIEW: RejectDirectSend = True closes the unauthenticated Direct Send SMTP endpoint that attackers abuse to spoof internal senders without credentials (bypassing SPF/DKIM/DMARC). Default is off — this is deliberate hardening.

CAUTION: enabling this can break legitimate unauthenticated internal send from devices/apps (printers, scanners, line-of-business apps). Since it's already on, confirm the client's migration to authenticated SMTP / a connector was completed — watch for 550 5.7.68 rejection errors indicating something was missed.
PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object FindTime*.
Not email security — permissive default posture across all four settings, nothing hardened. Documented for completeness; record drift only.

requiredSettings.label: Deployment mode
requiredSettings.value: Local (PublicFoldersEnabled: Local) — no on-prem hybrid dependency

requiredSettings.label: Retention / quotas
requiredSettings.value: Deleted 30d | Moved 7d | Warning 1.7GB | Prohibit post 2GB | MaxItemSize Unlimited

steps: In the Exchange admin center, go to Public folders for the folder list; org-wide defaults are read via PowerShell.
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object PublicFoldersEnabled, RemotePublicFolderMailboxes.
Local mode + no remote mailboxes confirms the tenant has no on-prem Exchange hybrid dependency for public folders (corroborates the Hybrid / Directory Sync row).

requiredSettings.label: Shorten event scope (Speedy meetings)
requiredSettings.value: None — staged but not applied org-wide (ShortenEventScopeDefault: None)

steps: PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object ShortenEventScopeDefault, DefaultMinutesToReduceShortEventsBy, DefaultMinutesToReduceLongEventsBy.
Not email security. HybridRSVPEnabled and MatchSenderOrganizerProperties have no documented Microsoft Learn description — report as configured only, don't interpret.
In the Microsoft 365 admin center, go to Settings > Org settings > Microsoft 365 Groups and review the defaults.
Verify in PowerShell: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object DefaultGroupAccessType, DistributionGroupNamingPolicy.

DefaultGroupAccessType = Private is the one access-control-relevant setting here and it's correctly conservative. Several other fields in this row are name-based inference, not confirmed docs — verify directly if any matters to a client conversation.

requiredSettings.label: Recall already-read messages
requiredSettings.value: True — but see the export discrepancy note

steps: In the Exchange admin center, go to Mail flow > Message recall to review the org setting.
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object MessageRecallEnabled, RecallReadMessagesEnabled, MessageRecallMaxRecallableAge.

EXPORT DISCREPANCY: a second export of this same policy (row 'Message Recall (Organization Config export)') shows RecallReadMessagesEnabled as null instead of True, and there's no confirmed Microsoft default for that specific field — resolve with the live Get-OrganizationConfig check above. Recall does not work cross-org, so it's not a DLP control if asked.
In the Exchange admin center, go to Settings > Mail flow (plus addressing checkbox).
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object DisablePlusAddressInRecipients, SendFromAliasEnabled.
Plus addressing is receive-side only per Microsoft docs — it can't be used to send, so there's no spoofing / send-as risk despite being enabled. Not a security-relevant setting.
In the Exchange admin center, the Reply-all storm protection report is under Reports > Mail flow (report view only); configuration is via PowerShell.
Verify: Get-TransportConfig | Select-Object ReplyAllStormProtectionEnabled, ReplyAllStormDetectionMinimumRecipients, ReplyAllStormDetectionMinimumReplies, ReplyAllStormBlockDurationHours.
These match Microsoft's current platform defaults exactly — inherited default behavior, not an Inforcer customization. Closest thing to an availability/resilience control in this set.

requiredSettings.label: Staff details exposed on booking page

steps: In the Microsoft 365 admin center, go to Settings > Org settings > Bookings and review the settings.
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object Bookings* .

PRIVACY CALLOUT: with these values, staff names/availability are publicly visible to anyone with the booking link and staff were never required to opt in. Standard for a customer scheduling tool, but confirm staff are aware — setting BookingsMembershipApprovalRequired = True is a one-setting fix if not.

requiredSettings.label: Notification recipients
requiredSettings.value: Empty — goes to ALL admins by default (not a gap)

steps: PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object ExchangeNotificationEnabled, ExchangeNotificationRecipients.
The empty recipients list is NOT a gap — per Microsoft docs, notifications go to all administrators when the field isn't set.
This is a legacy/narrow channel — distinct from Message Center, Service Health, and Defender alert policies. Don't treat it as the client's primary admin-alerting backbone.
Verify auditing in PowerShell: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object AuditDisabled (False = auditing on; confirms the Phase 6 checklist item).
For Customer Lockbox, go to the Microsoft 365 admin center: Settings > Org settings > Security & privacy > Customer lockbox, or Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object CustomerLockboxEnabled.

SKU-ENTITLEMENT FLAG: Customer Lockbox requires Microsoft 365 / Office 365 E5 or an Advanced Compliance add-on — NOT included in Business Premium. The toggle exists regardless of license and doesn't warn about a missing entitlement, so it may be non-functional as configured. Confirm the client's licensing so they don't have a false sense of protection.

requiredSettings.label: Recipient delimiters / Shared-domain flow
requiredSettings.value: Configured — both undocumented in Microsoft's reference

steps: PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object ByteEncoderTypeFor7BitCharsets, InRegionRoutingEnabled.

DUPLICATE FIELDS: DisablePlusAddressInRecipients and SendFromAliasEnabled appear here too but are the same fields already covered under 'Mail Flow General Settings' — an Inforcer baseline structure quirk, not a conflict.
RecipientDelimiters and SharedDomainEmailAddressFlowEnabled show blank descriptions in Microsoft's own reference — genuinely undocumented, report as configured only.

requiredSettings.label: Actionable Messages external access token
requiredSettings.value: False — legacy EAT auth, retired by Microsoft 2026-06-08 (likely a no-op)

steps: In the Microsoft 365 admin center, go to Settings > Org settings > Outlook to review the org-wide Outlook preferences.
Verify individual fields in PowerShell: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object TwoClickMailPreviewEnabled, PublicComputersDetectionEnabled, ActionableMessagesExtenalAccessTokenEnabled.

Mostly pure UX. TwoClickMailPreviewEnabled and PublicComputersDetectionEnabled are plausibly security-adjacent but have no confirmed Microsoft Learn description — treat as informational. The Actionable Messages EAT field is likely a no-op now (see 'Connectors & Actionable Messages').
PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object OutlookMobileGCCRestrictionsEnabled, OutlookPayEnabled, WorkspaceTenantEnabled.
No security relevance. The GCC restriction is moot on a commercial tenant; WorkspaceTenantEnabled likely maps to Microsoft Places (desk booking) but that mapping isn't fully confirmed.
PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object AutodiscoverPartialDirSync.
False confirms the tenant is fully cloud-only, not mid-hybrid-migration — corroborates the Public Folders row (Local mode, no remote mailboxes).
No single unified portal page — Connectors are managed per surface (Outlook > Manage add-ins; Teams admin center for Teams connectors).
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object ConnectorsEnabled, ConnectorsActionableMessagesEnabled, SmtpActionableMessagesEnabled.

Actionable Messages security is sound: providers must register with Microsoft and pass DKIM/SPF or signed-card verification before cards render interactive — 'enabled' doesn't mean any email can carry a working button. Legacy EAT token auth was fully retired 2026-06-08. Connectors being off may be deliberate hardening or Microsoft's platform-wide Connectors retirement — can't distinguish from the export alone.

requiredSettings.label: Admin / end-user notifications
requiredSettings.value: False (auto-set to False while the feature is off)

steps: Enable/disable is PowerShell-only; the 'License removed recently' view is in the Microsoft 365 admin center under Active users.
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object DelayedDelicensingEnabled.

Microsoft built this to delay mailbox-access removal by up to 30 days after a license is removed, so admins can catch accidental bulk delicensing before data loss. Worth a direct question: has this book of business had a bulk-delicensing incident, or is change management rigorous enough that it isn't needed? It costs nothing to enable if there's real risk.
PowerShell only (no confirmed portal page). Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object EwsApplicationAccessPolicy, EwsBlockList.
IMPORTANT CONTEXT: Microsoft states application-level EWS blocking is NOT a security feature — user-agent strings are trivially spoofed; credential auth remains the real boundary.

MacOutlook/* blocks ALL Mac Outlook versions with no version qualifier — possibly a stale mitigation from a 2015 CVE (patched long ago). Confirm with the client whether it's intentional and whether Mac users have silent functionality gaps.
Per-user archive enablement is in the Exchange admin center: Recipients > Mailboxes > (user) > Others > Manage mailbox archive. Org thresholds are read via PowerShell.
Verify: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object AutoExpandingArchiveEnabled; and the archiving threshold / AutoEnableArchiveMailbox settings.

INTERACTION FINDING: auto-archiving can't provision an archive mailbox itself — one must already exist. With AutoEnableArchiveMailbox = False, the 96% safety net is only live for users who already have an archive enabled; others risk send/receive failures once full. Run Get-Mailbox to check how many mailboxes lack an archive before deciding if this is a live risk.
Same underlying object as 'Mail Flow Message Recall Settings' (id message-recall), exported a second time under Inforcer's Organization Config category.

Verify the unresolved field directly: Get-OrganizationConfig | Select-Object RecallReadMessagesEnabled — one export shows True, this one shows null, and there's no confirmed Microsoft default for this specific field.
Kept as a separate row to match how the Inforcer baseline represents it; use the live value from the cmdlet as the source of truth.
